questions.description: Assess whether the EC Treaty, as interpreted in the case-law of the Community Courts, establishes a discernible…
questions.text: Assess whether the EC Treaty, as interpreted in the case-law of the Community Courts, establishes a discernible separation of legislative and executive powers between the Commission, the Council of Ministers and the European Parliament. What reforms, if any, would you suggest in this regard?

questions.description: With reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law, outline the composition, mode of appointment and…
questions.text: With reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law, outline the composition, mode of appointment and jurisdiction of the Court of Justice and the Court of First Instance. How has the Courts' Treaty-given role of "ensuring that in the interpretation and application of the Treaty, the law is observed" influenced the development of its jurisdiction?

questions.description: "Fundamental rights are treated in a paradoxical manner.
questions.text: "Fundamental rights are treated in a paradoxical manner. On one hand, EU law stresses the indivisibility of fundamental rights. Civil, political, economic rights are to be equally valued and protected. On the other, it does not protect their indefeasibility. Fundamental rights are only to be protected insofar as they fall within the field of EC law" Chalmers et al, European Union Law (Cambridge, 2006), at pp. 268/9. Evaluate this claim in light of the protection of fundamental rights in relevant Treaty provisions and in the case-law of the Community Courts.

questions.description: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following cases, appraising, inter alia, the significance of each chosen…
questions.text: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following cases, appraising, inter alia, the significance of each chosen case for the effectiveness of EC law: (i) Case 6/64 Costa v ENEL [1964] ECR 585; (ii) Case 41/74 Van Duyn [1974] ECR 1337; (iii) Cases C-6/90 and C-9/90 Francovich [1991] ECR I-5537; (iv) Case C-91/92 Faccini Dori [1994] ECR I-3325; (v) Case C-377/98 Netherlands v Parliament and Council (Biotechnology Directive) [2001] ECR I-7079; (vi) Case C-105/03 Pupino [2005] ECR I-5285.

questions.description: The Attorney General of Ireland has asked you to advise on the…
questions.text: The Attorney General of Ireland has asked you to advise on the compatibility of the following proposed measures with EC law, in particular Articles 25 EC, 28 EC, 49 EC and 90 EC: (i) A scientific advisory body has recommended to the Government that all bread manufactured or marketed in Ireland should be required to be fortified with folic acid. The body believes that such a measure would reduce the number of babies born in Ireland with neural tube defects, such as spina bifida. Evidence from a number of third world countries suggests that such a mandatory fortification policy is effective. The Department of Health and Children is considering the introduction of such mandatory fortification. (ii) The Bord na gCon (Irish Greyhound Board) has proposed to the Department of Agriculture and Food that a 1% levy be introduced and imposed on the sale, both for the domestic and international markets, of greyhounds raised in Ireland. 60% of the revenues generated from the levy are to be used by Bord na gCon to provide financial assistance towards the upgrading of facilities at greyhound racing stadia around the country on foot of approved development plans submitted by their owners, while the remaining 40% is to be used to finance the promotion of the sale of Irish greyhounds in other Member States, especially in the United Kingdom. (iii) The Department of Justice, Equality and Law Reform, concerned at what it perceives to be increasingly aggressive behaviour by young people, has proposed to the Minister for Justice, Equality and Law Reform that he consider introducing a prohibition on the sale, rental or exploitation (in games halls, amusement arcades, public houses and youth centres) of any computer games that involve the participants mimicking violence, in particular those that involve the mimicking of shooting with firearms. Set out your advice in [three]{.underline} separate and succinct opinions.

questions.description: A partner in your law firm asks you to provide legal advice for her… Advise Heidi on whether she is eligible for this…
questions.text: A partner in your law firm asks you to provide legal advice for her perusal in respect of [each]{.underline} of the following [three]{.underline} clients. Set out your advice in three separate and succinct opinions. (i) [Heidi]{.underline}, a German national who has been living as a new age traveller in west Cork, joins a sect called the 'Screeching Loolahs'. All members of the sect are required to work for about 12 hours per week in the sect's coffee shop in Bantry. She spends the rest of her time practising meditation and screeching. The sect encourages Heidi to apply for a training allowance available to workers who retrain on returning (after an extended absence) to the workforce. Advise Heidi on whether she is eligible for this grant. (ii) [Ella]{.underline}, an Italian national, has been living and working in Ireland for over two years. She recently married Vladamir in Moscow. Vladamir is a Russian national and an old flame of Ella's from her days in the 1990s as a student of Slavic languages in St Petersburg. Advise Ella and Vladamir on whether Vladamir has a right under EC law as Ella's husband to enter and reside in Ireland. (iii) [Jacques]{.underline} is a French national who moved to Dublin after the death of his parents in a car crash when he was sixteen to live with his grandmother, who is also a French national and a professional freelance musician. After completing his leaving certificate two years later, Jacques applied for a local authority maintenance grant to attend university from Dublin City Council. Although his grandmother's income and his own income from his inheritance were lower than the cut-off threshold for the grant, Jacques was refused a grant on the basis that he did not satisfy the residency requirement, pursuant to which five years continuous residence is required on the date of application. Jacques has heard that the requirement is occasionally waived by the City Council, in its absolute discretion, for applicants of Irish nationality who have resided abroad for fewer than three of the five years required, provided the move abroad occurred because one or both of the applicant's parents (or guardians) moved to work abroad.

Answer part (a) [or]{.underline} (b). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (a) Ellas Rapid Constructions is a Greek construction company that has won, through an Irish subsidiary, a tender project to build a new motorway in County Galway. Through its Irish subsidiary, Rapid Constructions (Dublin) Ltd, it seeks to recruit most of the skilled workers that it will need to carry out the project for the National Roads Authority. Its Irish subsidiary is coming under "pressure" from certain local trade unions to recruit two Irish workers for every one non-Irish EU citizen on the project. The unions have also indicated that they will organise an industrial boycott of the project if, as proposed in Ellas' successful tender, 50% of the skilled construction workers needed are posted to Ireland from Greece. According to the call for tenders, at least 50% of the general labourers required to complete the project should "as far as practicable be drawn from the workforce of the West of Ireland, or from persons on the liver register and registered as resident in the West of Ireland region" , which region is defined as including the counties of the province of Connaught". As a specialist in EC law, Rapid Constructions (Dublin) Ltd has sought your advice as to whether: (i) if it were to limit the number of skilled positions available to non-Irish EU citizens on the project, it would infringe any provisions of the EC Treaty or secondary legislation; (ii) the threatened boycott of the trade unions could be successfully challenged on the basis of the EC Treaty before the Irish courts; (iii) it could legitimately refuse, on the basis of Articles 43 EC and/or 49 EC, to respect the tender stipulation regarding the engagement of general labourers. Set out your advice. -- OR -- (b) "Article 141 (1) EC lays down the principle that equal work or work of equal value must be remunerated in the same way, whether it is performed by a man or a woman [...and] that principle, which is a particular expression of the general principle of equality which prohibits comparable situations from being treated differently unless the difference is objectively justified, forms a part of the foundations of the Community" (see Case C-17/05 Cadman [2006] ECR I-9583, at paragraphs 27 and 28). Discuss the contribution of the Court of Justice in its case-law on equal pay under Article 141 EC (formerly Article 119 EC) and related legislation to the goal of equality between men and women.

Answer part (a) [or]{.underline} (b). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (a) "There is nothing, in principle, to prevent two or more independent economic entities from being, on a specific market, united by such economic links that, by virtue of that fact, together they hold a dominant position vis-à-vis the other operators on the same market. This could be the case, for example, where two or more independent undertakings jointly have, through agreements or licences, a technological lead affording them the power to behave to an appreciable extent independently of their customers and ultimately of their consumers" (Joined Cases T-68, T-77 and T-78/89 Italian Flat Glass [1992] ECR II-1403, paragraph 358). How has the Community judicature developed the abovementioned principle of collective (or joint) dominance since the judgment of the Court of First Instance in Italian Flat Glass? -- OR -- (b) It has been argued that the Community Courts have "apparently been willing to apply Article [82] EC where there is no effective abuse by undertakings of a dominant position, but where a State measure produces the same effect" (Bacon, "State Regulation of the Market and EC Competition Rules" (1997) ECLRev. 283). Having regard to the case-law of the Community Courts interpreting Article 86 EC, assess critically the limits to the scope of Article 86 EC.

questions.description: "The story of the European Parliament is one of gradual transformation…
questions.text: "The story of the European Parliament is one of gradual transformation from a relatively powerless Assembly under the 1952 ECSC Treaty to the considerably strengthened institution it represents today" (Craig & De Burca, EU Law: Texts, Cases and Materials, (Oxford, OUP, 4td edition), 2007, p.58). By reference to, inter alia, Treaty provisions and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice, analyse this statement with particular reference to: (i) the evolution of the European Parliament's role in the EC legislative process; (ii) the features that distinguish its legislative role from that on any national parliament.

questions.description: On 1 February 2008, West County Council, a local authority in the… Damages, Directive.
questions.text: On 1 February 2008, West County Council, a local authority in the (fictitious) EU Member State of Boomtonia, granted Windy Ltd 'special planning permission' ('SPP') for construction of a windfarm in West County. West County Council did not carry out an 'environmental detriment assessment' ('EDA') in respect of the application, as none was required under the planning law of Boomtonia. On 1 July 2008, the ECJ decided in Commission v Boomtonia that the Boomtonian planning practice of granting SPP without EDA was contrary to the (fictitious) EC EDA Directive. The Green Alliance, a group of local residents opposed to the development, wish to have the national court quash West County Council's grant of SPP. In addition, Mr Farmer would like compensation for the reduction in the value of his property occasioned by the construction of the windfarm, which has rendered his farm prone to landslides in times of heavy rain. Advise the Green Alliance on whether and in what circumstances: (i) it may rely on the EC EDA Directive against West County Council in order to have grant of SPP quashed; (ii) it may rely on the EC EDA Directive against Windy Ltd to stop the development. Advise Mr Farmer on whether: (iii) he is entitled to damages from Boomtonia for its breach of EC law. Would it make any difference to your answer if West County Council's decision had been taken on 1 August 2008? (iv) he is entitled to damages from Windy Ltd under EU law.

questions.description: Discuss by reference to relevant case-law of the Court of Justice: (i) the interpretation of the term "court or…
questions.text: Discuss by reference to relevant case-law of the Court of Justice: (i) the interpretation of the term "court or tribunal of a Member State" for the purposes of the second paragraph of Article 234 EC; (ii) the interpretation of the term "court or tribunal of a Member State against whose decisions there is no judicial remedy under national law" for the purposes of the third paragraph of Article 234 EC; (iii) the legal effects of a preliminary ruling from the Court of Justice under Article 234 EC for, firstly, the referring court and, secondly, for other national courts or tribunals.

questions.description: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out for each case, the relevant facts, key EC legal issues…
questions.text: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out for each case, the relevant facts, key EC legal issues and how the ECJ addressed those issues. (i) Case 106/11 Simmenthal [1978] ECR 629; (ii) Case 44/79 Hauer v Rheinland-Pfalz [1979] ECR 3727; (iii) Case C-376/98 Germany v European Parliament and Council [2000] ECR I-8419; (iv) Case C-50/00 P Unión de Pequeños Agricultores v Council [2003] ECR I-6677; (v) Case C-280/00 Altmark Trans and Regierunspräsidium Magdeburg [2003] ECR I- 7747; (vi) Case C-224/01 Köbler [2003] ECR I-10239.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of each of the following measures and practices with Articles 10 EC, 28 to 30 EC and/or 49 to…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of each of the following measures and practices with Articles 10 EC, 28 to 30 EC and/or 49 to 50 EC, as applicable: (i) Irish animal rights activists routinely blockade ports to hinder the export of live cattle to Spain, as they believe that the trade embodies cruelty to animals. The Irish authorities invariably permit the protests on the basis that they are an exercise of the fundamental right to protest under Irish law; (ii) A Swedish trade union is boycotting the provision of building services by an Estonian building contractor, because the Estonian building contractor is paying its workers less than the minimum wage agreed in the collective agreement that binds Swedish employers and trade unions in the building industry. Swedish labour law permits trade unions to engage in this sort of collective action, in order to protect the collectively agreed Swedish minimum wage, wherever an employer, whether Swedish or foreign, has not accepted the relevant collective agreement; (iii) The law in Finland prohibits all advertisement of alcoholic beverages available for sale in the traditional or electronic media, unless the alcoholic content of the beverage is less than 2.5%. Finland argues the measure is justified on public health grounds. Set out your advice in respect of each law.

Answer parts (i), (ii) and (iii). (i) John, an Irish citizen, has been accepted onto a course of study at a Belgian university. The Belgian authorities refuse his application for the student maintenance grant provided for Belgian students, on the basis that he has not been resident in Belgium for 5 years before commencing his course of study. Belgium argues that its refusal is permitted under Article 24 of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States. John seeks your advice as to whether the refusal is lawful under EC law. (ii) Mairead is an Irish national, who lived and worked in the United Kingdom for several years before returning to Ireland last year, whereafter she met and married Hanam, a failed asylum seeker from Afghanistan. The Minister for Justice, Equality and Law Reform recently served a notice of intention to deport of Hanam. Mairead has asked for advice as to whether Hanam has an EC legal right to reside in Ireland as her spouse. Ireland argues that Directive 2004/38/EC does not apply as Mairead is an Irish citizen resident in Ireland. (iii) Roman, an Italian national holding a doctorate in German language and literature, has been refused employment with a private financial institution in Bolzano, the German-speaking region of Northern Italy, on the sole basis that he does not hold the requisite official certificate of bilingualism in Italian and German issued by the Bolzano ministry of education. Roman seeks your advice as to whether the refusal to engage him is compatible with EC law. Set out your advice to John, Mairead and Roman. Note that each of the scenarios arises after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States.

questions.description: Advise Joan [and]{.underline} Mary in the following situations: (a) Joan… Regulation.
questions.text: Answer part (a) or (b). Do not attempt both parts. Advise Joan [and]{.underline} Mary in the following situations: (a) Joan has worked as a manager for a health authority for the past five years. Previously, following her graduation from university, she worked in the private sector for several years before becoming a homemaker for a period of six years. She has discovered that a number of male colleagues, who hold similar managerial positions to Joan for periods of between two and four years, are all paid more than her. In response to Joan's complaint, the health authority argues that the differences in pay are justified by the male comparators' longer service records with the authority, albeit mostly at lower grades than Joan. Advise Joan as to the lawfulness of the authority's use of the criterion of length of service as a detriment of pay under EC sex discrimination law, in particular Article 141 EC. Mary is a waitress working for a restaurant on a 6-month temporary contract of employment. Just as her contract is due to expire, she discovers that she is pregnant. On informing her employer, he decides not to renew her contract on its expiry. As Mary would be taking maternity leave in the following 6 months, he feels she would be unable to fulfil the terms of a new contract. Advise Mary as to whether the refusal to renew her contract under these circumstances is contrary to EC sex discrimination law. OR (b) Consider whether the Brussels Convention of 27^th^ September 1968 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters, as now largely replaced by Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters ("the Brussels I Regulation") establishes clear jurisdictional rules for determining which courts have jurisdiction in civil and commercial matters involving inter-state elements. In your answer refer, in particular, to the scope and operation of the principal jurisdiction rule under Article 2(1) of Regulation No. 44/2001 and the special rules laid down by Article 5(1) and (3).

questions.description: "[...] Price competition is the essence of free and open competition which is the objective of Community policy to…
questions.text: "[...] Price competition is the essence of free and open competition which is the objective of Community policy to establish on the internal market. It favours more efficient firms and it is for the benefit of consumers both in the short and long run. Dominant firms not only have the right but should be encouraged to compete on price" (Opinion of Advocate General Fennelly in Case C-395/96 P Compagnie Maritime Belge v Commission [2000] ECR I-1365. Discuss this statement with particular reference to the case-law of the Community judicature relating to the constraints placed by Article 82 EC on the pricing activities (such as concerning predatory pricing, price squeezing and excessive prices) of dominant firms.
